skill.name: ai-image-generation
skill.description: Generate and edit AI images through the inference.sh belt CLI using GPT-Image-2, FLUX, Gemini, Grok, Seedream, Reve, and related image apps.
skill.summary: Run inference.sh image generation and editing models from Claude, Codex, or Claude Code.
skill.author: skills-shell

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many external command and URL patterns plus one weak-cryptography pattern. Review found the command findings are mostly Markdown examples for the expected belt CLI workflow, and the weak-cryptography finding at SKILL.md line 3 appears to be a false positive with no cryptographic code present. The skill still carries medium risk because it instructs agents to run a networked CLI that sends prompts and image URLs to third-party inference services.

medium_findings.title: Networked CLI Sends Prompts and Image URLs to External Services
medium_findings.description: The skill is designed around belt app run commands that call hosted inference.sh apps. This is legitimate for image generation, but user prompts and image URLs may be transmitted to third-party model providers, so users should avoid secrets, private images, and confidential brand assets unless policy permits it.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented commands invoke remote inference apps and include examples that pass prompts and image URLs. No malicious exfiltration intent was found, but external data transfer is central to the workflow.

medium_findings.title: Supply Chain Exposure From Skill Installation Commands
medium_findings.description: The documentation recommends installing CLI and related skills with npx skills add commands. This can be normal setup, but it introduces trust risk because users may fetch executable tooling or additional community skills from external repositories.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The installation commands are explicit and external. They appear related to the skill purpose, but users should review source and package provenance before running them.

low_findings.title: Static Backtick Execution Findings Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The analyzer reported many Ruby or shell backtick execution findings, but the reviewed locations are Markdown inline code, command examples, or code fences. They are not executable code inside the skill file, although the commands may be run by an agent when the user requests image generation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is Markdown documentation, and the flagged backticks are formatting delimiters or examples. There is no Ruby source or automatic command execution in the reviewed content.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Finding Is Not Supported by File Context
low_findings.description: The analyzer flagged SKILL.md line 3 for weak cryptography, but that line is frontmatter describing image models and trigger phrases. No evidence of cryptographic code, hashing, encryption, or credential handling was found at that location.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 contains descriptive metadata only. The weak cryptography classification does not match the observed content.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation and Example Inputs
low_findings.description: The URLs point to inference.sh documentation, an image asset, install instructions, and placeholder image inputs. They do not show covert endpoints or automatic credential exfiltration, but they confirm that the skill depends on external network services.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation links or sample image fields. No hidden data collection or suspicious destination was found in the reviewed file.

dangerous_patterns.title: Allowed Bash Access for belt Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill frontmatter allows Bash commands matching belt. This is necessary for the skill, but it permits networked external process execution through the belt CLI.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools entry is explicit and scoped to belt. The risk is clear but tied to the advertised functionality.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Image Generation Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples run remote model apps with user-provided prompts and optional image URLs. This can expose prompt text or image references to external services.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Multiple examples use belt app run against hosted model IDs. The reviewed content does not hide the network behavior.

content.user_title: Create AI Images With Inference Models
content.value_statement: Teams need fast image generation without memorizing model-specific commands. This skill provides ready belt CLI workflows for image creation, editing, upscaling, and model discovery.
actual_capabilities: Runs inference.sh image apps through the belt CLI.
Shows text-to-image commands for GPT-Image-2, FLUX, Gemini, Grok, Reve, and Seedream.
Provides examples for image editing, upscaling, and stitching with image URLs.
Lists model IDs and suggested uses for more than a dozen image apps.
Includes commands to browse additional image apps from the CLI.
limitations: Requires the belt CLI and an authenticated inference.sh account.
Sends prompts and image URLs to external model services.
Does not validate provider pricing, quotas, or model availability.
Does not provide local image generation or offline model execution.

use_cases.title: Create Product Mockups
use_cases.description: Generate studio-style product visuals from concise prompts for early concept review.

use_cases.title: Explore Visual Concepts
use_cases.description: Try several model families and aspect ratios before selecting a final art direction.

use_cases.title: Prepare Social Media Assets
use_cases.description: Create, edit, upscale, and combine image assets from repeatable CLI commands.

prompt_templates.title: Generate a Simple Image
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate an image of a modern desk setup with natural light. Use a reliable model and a square aspect ratio.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Product Visual
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a professional product photo of a reusable water bottle on a clean studio background. Use high quality settings.

prompt_templates.title: Edit an Existing Image
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this image URL and change the background to a bright kitchen scene. Keep the product shape unchanged.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Model Options
prompt_templates.prompt: Recommend two image models for a cinematic poster with readable title text. Explain the tradeoffs and run the best option.

output_examples.input: Generate a studio product image for sneakers.
output_examples.output: The assistant selects a suitable image model, runs a belt command, and returns the generated image link with the prompt used.

output_examples.input: Upscale this image URL for a presentation.
output_examples.output: The assistant runs the upscaler app with the provided URL and returns the enhanced image result.
The assistant lists image apps from the belt store and suggests models for quality, speed, editing, or text rendering.

best_practices: Review prompts for confidential data before sending them to external providers.
Use specific subject, style, lighting, and aspect ratio details in each prompt.
Confirm model cost, license, and output rights before production use.
anti_patterns: Do not submit private images or secrets unless your policy allows external processing.
Do not run install commands from unfamiliar repositories without reviewing source trust.
Do not assume every listed model is available, current, or priced the same.

faq.question: What does this skill use to generate images?
faq.answer: It uses the inference.sh belt CLI to run hosted image generation, editing, and upscaling apps.

faq.question: Does it work in Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Do I need an account?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill documentation says belt login is required before running image apps.

faq.question: Can it edit existing images?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples include image editing, upscaling, and stitching workflows using image URLs.

faq.question: Is it safe for confidential images?
faq.answer: Use caution. Prompts and image URLs may be sent to external inference services.

faq.question: Does it include local model files?
faq.answer: No. It documents remote CLI commands and does not include local model weights.
